turns.content: I'm trying to understand string interning in Rust, and I found the official doc confusing. Can you explain how arena-backed interning works?

Sure! So, imagine you have a lot of strings that might repeat in your app—like file paths or identifiers—and you want to save memory by deduplicating them. One approach is using an arena allocator (like bumpalo) combined with a HashMap.
Oh, so it's like creating a big pool of strings and then looking up duplicates there? But why do I need an arena? Isn't that just allocating memory?

Exactly! The arena allocator (say bumpalo::Bump) is great because it allocates memory all at once in a contiguous block, which makes deallocation simpler. Plus, you can keep everything allocated together.
Wait, so I should always use an arena? What if I just use HashMaps with Rc<str>? That seems simpler.

Good point! Using Rc<str> and a HashMap is straightforward. But remember that every time you deduplicate strings, you create new Rc<str>s, which can be costly. An arena-backed approach avoids that by reusing memory from the pool.
